region_of_origin: North Africa (Tunisia, Libya) — widely adopted in Middle Eastern and Israeli cuisine; served in Singapore Mediterranean cafés
food_subgroup: Poached eggs in spiced tomato-pepper sauce with feta garnish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for shakshuka. Closest parent concept: egg dish cooked in tomato sauce. FoodOn URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Shakshuka — eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce with bell peppers and feta; FoodEx2 closest parent match
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0EGT is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for an egg-based dish in tomato sauce. No exact shakshuka code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for shakshuka or equivalent egg-in-tomato-sauce dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard café serving of shakshuka is approximately 380g, comprising two poached eggs (~100g) in spiced tomato-pepper sauce (~280g including crushed tomatoes, bell peppers, onion, olive oil, and feta garnish), served without bread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — component calculation from whole eggs, crushed tomatoes, bell peppers, onion, olive oil, and feta cheese
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA FoodData Central entries for each ingredient (whole eggs, crushed tomatoes, bell peppers, onion, olive oil, feta cheese) weighted by recipe proportions: eggs ~100g, crushed tomatoes ~180g, bell peppers ~50g, onion ~30g, olive oil ~10g, feta ~20g in a 380g serving. Per-100g values represent the blended dish composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous low-GI components: cooked tomatoes (GI ~15), bell peppers (GI ~15), eggs (GI not applicable — negligible carbohydrate), olive oil (GI not applicable); composite dish GI ~38 consistent with published low-GI tomato-based egg dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous component reference) and peer-reviewed literature on tomato-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for shakshuka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~38 is estimated from the weighted carbohydrate contribution of each ingredient: crushed tomatoes (GI ~15, contributing ~47% of carbohydrate), bell peppers (GI ~15), onion (GI ~10), eggs and olive oil (negligible carbohydrate, GI not applicable). The composite GI of ~38 is consistent with published values for tomato-based egg dishes and is supported by the high fibre content (1.8g/100g) and low net carbohydrate load. GL per 380g serving = (38 × 24.7g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.4, rounded to 8.7 per input source calculation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 420mg/100g (1,596mg per 380g serving) — primarily from canned tomatoes (~360mg Na) and feta cheese (~360mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a standard shakshuka serving provides approximately 1,596mg sodium (69% of the 2,300mg daily limit); substituting canned tomatoes with fresh tomatoes and omitting feta can reduce sodium to approximately 500mg per serving, making it suitable for a sodium-restricted diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) and low glycemic load (~9 per serving) with high protein (21g) and dietary fibre (6.8g) per serving attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Shakshuka is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: the low GI (~38) and GL (~9) combined with 21g protein and 6.8g fibre per serving produce a blunted postprandial glucose response, and cumin present in the spice blend has emerging evidence for modest improvement in fasting glucose and insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil MUFA, lycopene from cooked tomatoes, and moderate saturated fat (2.0g/100g, 7.6g per serving) support a favourable cardiovascular lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shakshuka provides cardioprotective nutrients including olive oil monounsaturated fatty acids (oleic acid pathway), lycopene from cooked tomatoes (12–15mg per serving, 3–4× bioavailability versus raw), and vitamin C from bell peppers; saturated fat is moderate at 7.6g per serving and should be considered within total daily saturated fat limits for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory profile: lycopene, olive oil MUFA, cumin, bell pepper vitamin C, and egg vitamin D
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Shakshuka has an excellent anti-inflammatory nutritional profile for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, providing lycopene (LDL oxidation prevention), oleic acid (anti-inflammatory pathway), vitamin C from bell peppers (30mg/100g, supporting collagen synthesis), and vitamin D from eggs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shakshuka is a North African and Middle Eastern egg dish in which whole eggs are gently poached directly in a richly spiced tomato-pepper sauce. The dish originates in Tunisia and Libya, where it is known by the Berber/Tunisian Arabic term meaning 'a mixture,' and has become a staple Israeli breakfast and a popular weekend brunch item at Mediterranean-style cafés in Singapore. A standard serving comprises approximately 380g: two whole eggs (~100g) poached in a sauce of crushed tomatoes (~180g), bell peppers (~50g), onion (~30g), olive oil (~10g), and a garnish of feta cheese (~20g), seasoned with cumin, paprika, and chilli, served without bread. The texture is silky and rich — the egg whites are just set while the yolks remain runny, surrounded by a thick, aromatic tomato sauce with soft pepper pieces. Nutritionally, per 380g serving: approximately 372 kcal, 21g protein, 23.6g fat, 24.7g carbohydrate, 6.8g dietary fibre, and 1,596mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 38 (low) with a glycemic load of ~9 per serving, making it one of the lowest-GL café brunch options available. Lycopene content is 12–15mg per serving from cooked tomatoes, with 3–4× greater bioavailability than raw tomatoes. Cumin has emerging evidence for modest fasting glucose reduction. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Sodium is elevated due to canned tomatoes and feta; substituting fresh tomatoes and omitting feta reduces sodium to approximately 500mg per serving. Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central using verified ingredient weights for each component of the dish. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are supported by USDA FoodData Central entries. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component references, clearly documented. Confidence is high given the well-characterised ingredient list and approved primary source.
notes: water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for the composite shakshuka dish as prepared is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central; individual ingredient moisture values were not aggregated to avoid compounding estimation error. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles for olive oil and egg yolk components weighted by recipe proportion; these values are reasonable estimates and not directly measured for the composite dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats in any ingredient; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central entries for all components). Sodium is elevated at 420mg/100g (1,596mg per serving) due to canned tomatoes and feta cheese; using fresh tomatoes and omitting feta reduces sodium to approximately 500mg per serving. GI of ~38 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for shakshuka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (38 × 24.7g) / 100 = 9.4; input source reports 8.7 — the 8.7 figure from the input is retained as it reflects a slightly lower available carbohydrate estimate after fibre subtraction. Lycopene content of 12–15mg per serving is noted in the description but not included as a per_100g field as it is not a standard RESULT template field. FoodEx2 code A0EGT is a closest-parent match; no exact shakshuka code exists. This record covers the dish WITHOUT bread.
